narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and a middle-class family background, you've put real effort into your law school journey, including planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 51% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your application—it's a close call, but the threshold requires stronger odds for approval. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance lagged behind peers who tend to pass the bar. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA, close to the dataset average of 3.21, strongly pushed the prediction toward success, showing your college record as a real strength. Your middle-income family background also helped nudge things positively compared to higher earners who sometimes face different pressures. Lesser influences included your Year 1 decile of 2, slightly below average and adding a small risk, while your full-time status and LSAT score of 14.50 provided minor boosts toward passing, aligning with most applicants. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile stands out for its average GPA but is held back by those lower decile rankings, especially in Year 3, which outweigh the positives in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your specifics stacked up. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, aiming for top-5 rankings that signal bar-exam readiness to models like this. You might also explore LSAT prep to lift that score closer to the 36.70 average, or seek admissions appeals highlighting any extenuating circumstances from your Year 3 performance. Many applicants in similar spots improve and succeed—keep pushing, and law school could still be yours. We're rooting for you.
